retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The clinicians should perform a clinical evaluation to exclude these conditions before proceeding with the brain death examination. It is essential to differentiate between coma and brain death. When the AAN guidelines are followed, there have been no misdiagnoses of brain death reported in the literature; of the reported misdiagnoses, at least one violation of the AAN guidelines was identified. [10] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Once brain death has been diagnosed, the patient is clinically and legally declared deceased at the time of completion of brain death testing. At this time, depending on family and patient preference, cardiopulmonary support should be withdrawn, or arrangements for organ harvest should commence. Adequate documentation of all criteria of the diagnosis of brain death must be included in the patient's medical record. It is recommended that some form of checklist be used to ensure the completeness of brain death testing. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death Criteria (Archived) -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Time of death is also country and institutional guideline-based. As per AAN, the time of death is when PCO2 reaches the target value during the apnea test. For patients who underwent the ancillary examination, death is when the results are officially reported. [14] However, in the United Kingdom, although two exams are needed to diagnose the test formally, the legal time of death is when the first test confirms the absence of brainstem reflexes. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death Criteria (Archived) -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: A recent study, "The World Brain Death Project," tried to achieve the minimum clinical criteria needed to determine brain death by performing a literature search of important databases from January 1, 1992, through April 2020. [13] The study proposed brain death with the exam demonstrating coma, brainstem areflexia, and apnea. Ancillary testing, including blood-brain flow studies and electroencephalogram, can be used if a clinical exam is non-yielding.

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Although not required for the diagnosis of brain death, hospitals frequently have additional ancillary tests available to confirm the diagnosis of brain death. Ancillary tests are used when apnea testing is inconclusive or patients are too unstable to proceed with apnea testing. [16] [3] These tests include:

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death Criteria (Archived) -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: In the United States, the physician's eligibility to perform a brain death examination varies from state to state and institution to institution. Most states and institutions legally allow any physician to perform brain death exams, while some only allow neurologists, neurosurgeons, and intensive care specialists. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Coma should be evaluated by ensuring a lack of responsiveness to noxious stimuli; no eye or motor reflex should be present in response to stimuli. Spinal-mediated reflex movements, including muscle stretch reflexes, may be compatible with brain death. [14] Additionally, the cause of the coma should be identified by neuroimaging, history, and physical examination or laboratory testing.

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Requirements for the diagnosis of brain death are coma with an identified cause, absence of brainstem reflexes, and apnea. The diagnosis is best made by closely following the American Academy of Neurology guidelines. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Questioning the diagnosis of brain death only arises when AAN guidelines or protocols for brain death determination are not followed. These complications are limited to misdiagnosing a different neurologic condition as brain death and the ultimate death of a patient who was not brain dead. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The following cranial nerve (CN) and brainstem reflexes should be tested in the physical examination of a patient eligible for brain death evaluation. [15] They all must be absent for a patient to be diagnosed as brain dead:

retrieved_docs.title: Death Certification -- Other Issues -- Brain Death
retrieved_docs.content: Brain death is defined as the irreversible cessation of all functions of the brain activities, including the brain stem. Traditionally, the concept of death has always been historically linked to the cessation of heartbeat, but with advances in technology, including mechanical ventilation and inotropic support therapy, an individual might have a beating heart but an irreversible loss of brain functions. [11] [12] Brain death declaration is a wholly clinical diagnosis. Practitioners are not required to perform another test if 2 tests for brain stem function and an apnea test are conclusive for brain death.

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death Criteria (Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Death is defined in the United States by the Uniform Determination of Death Act (UDDA), proposed in 1981. A determination of death must be made in accordance with the accepted medical standards and must additionally include one of the following: Irreversible cessation of circulatory and pulmonary functions. Irreversible cessation of all functions of the entire brain, including the brainstem, is dead.

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Death is defined in the United States by the Uniform Determination of Death Act (UDDA), proposed in 1981. A determination of death must be made in accordance with the accepted medical standards and must additionally include one of the following: Irreversible cessation of circulatory and pulmonary functions. Irreversible cessation of all functions of the entire brain, including the brainstem, is dead.

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In 2012, the World Health Organization (WHO) partnered with an international forum to endorse brain death as the official diagnosis of death. [4] However, internationally and even within different states of the United States, there is no uniformity to certify brain death. Brain death criteria also differ based on the patient's age. Therefore, different standards are in effect when performing a document of brain death, and the provider should be aware of their country/state's criteria for a specific patient's age.

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death Criteria (Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In 2012, the World Health Organization (WHO) partnered with an international forum to endorse brain death as the official diagnosis of death. [4] However, internationally and even within different states of the United States, there is no uniformity to certify brain death. Brain death criteria also differ based on the patient's age. Therefore, different criteria are in effect to perform a document of brain death, and the provider should be cognizant of their country/state's criteria for a specific patient's age.

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death Criteria (Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: It is essential to distinguish the term "brain death" from "coma" to the public, as coma may imply a limited form of life. The understanding that brain death is equivalent to death helps guide decision-making for both physicians and patients' families regarding the withdrawal of care and prevents the unnecessary expenditure of resources. An essential topic that evolved in parallel with brain death is obtaining organs for transplantation. According to the "dead donor rule," organ procurement can occur only after death has been declared. Therefore, for patients who are brain dead, the procurement of viable organs is allowed, even if a patient still has some circulatory and/or pulmonary function. This concept continues to result in some ongoing debate and controversy. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2105
retrieved_docs.content: Subarachnoid hemorrhage following injury with open intracranial wound, unspecified state of consciousness is associated with إصابة داخلية (internal injury), indicating that this form of hemorrhagic brain trauma often co‑occurs with internal injuries and should be evaluated together in clinical assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1027
retrieved_docs.content: unspecified state of consciousness is associated with إصابة داخلية (internal injury), reinforcing the connection between severe intracranial bleeding and internal traumatic damage. Together, these relationships map a comprehensive hierarchy of general to specific injuries, illustrating how broad injury categories subdivide into distinct anatomical and pathological entities that are relevant for diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2015
retrieved_docs.content: Coma, stupor, and brain damage are clinical manifestations that frequently co‑occur with عَوَز الأكسجين, indicating that insufficient oxygen supply to the brain can lead to these severe neurological states.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: and secondary swelling of brain tissue. Together, these interconnections emphasize the centrality of the brain in diverse pathological, diagnostic, and therapeutic contexts, and they underscore the importance of recognizing overlapping disease categories for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 1174
retrieved_docs.content: each integrated into specific anatomical and procedural categories, frequently co‑occurring with head CT and abdominal CT, and complemented by CNS imaging modalities such as abnormal EEG to support thorough patient evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: اعتلال دماغي (brain pathology), indicating that acute brain injury is a specific type of brain pathology. Recognizing the enlarged Sylvian fissure, abnormal brain echo map, and the classification of acute brain injury under brain pathology is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 189
retrieved_docs.content: links imaging findings, anatomical structures, injury classifications, and interpretation tools, providing a comprehensive view of neurological injuries, vascular and inflammatory disorders, and related movement disorders essential for accurate diagnosis, coding, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2445
retrieved_docs.content: Other cerebrovascular diseases (Overige cerebrovasculaire ziekten) comprise a range of distinct conditions, including dissection of cerebral arteries without rupture (Dissectie van cerebrale arteriën zonder ruptuur) and brain injury caused by excessive blood pressure (اعتلال دماغي ناجم عن فَرْط ضَغْطِ الدَّم). Both of these entities are classified as child conditions of the broader category of other cerebrovascular diseases, illustrating the diversity of non‑hemorrhagic vascular pathologies that can affect the brain. Recognizing these specific subtypes within Overige cerebrovasculaire ziekten is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and targeted therapeutic strategies in cerebrovascular patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: developmental disorders, and traumatic brain injury constitute an interconnected spectrum of nervous‑system disorders that inform comprehensive diagnostic and therapeutic strategies in neurology and neurosurgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 207
retrieved_docs.content: are organized within injury classification frameworks, share common external cause codes, and often involve similar pathological outcomes such as tissue damage and neurotoxicity, providing a cohesive framework for assessment and management of traumatic patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 273
retrieved_docs.content: pathway. Additional neurosurgical considerations include إحصار عصبي عضلي (neuromuscular blockade) and the spectrum of related neuropathologies such as diffuse encephalitis around the axis (الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور). Overall, the interconnected knowledge emphasizes that استسقاء الدماغ is a central nervous system disorder with defined anatomical locations, characteristic clinical associations (especially with dementia), specific neuroimaging modalities (CT head and abnormal brain echo), and a set of neurosurgical interventions essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2226
retrieved_docs.content: Traumatisch hersenoedeem is a specific subtype of أوديما دماغية, meaning that traumatic brain edema is classified under the broader condition of cerebral edema. As a form of أوديما دماغية, Traumatisch hersenoedeem involves swelling of brain tissue that results from head injury, leading to increased intracranial pressure and potential neurological impairment. Recognizing Traumatisch hersenoedeem as a subset of أوديما دماغية helps clinicians understand its pathophysiology within the context of general cerebral edema, guiding appropriate diagnostic evaluation and targeted therapeutic interventions to reduce brain swelling and protect neurological function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2325
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral injury due to birth trauma is identified as a specific manifestation of birth trauma, indicating that brain injuries occurring during the birthing process are encompassed within the broader category of birth trauma. Recognizing this relationship highlights the importance of evaluating neurological complications when assessing and managing birth‑related injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2126
retrieved_docs.content: Transient cerebral ischemia is characterized by a transitory clinical course, indicating that the neurological deficits associated with this brief interruption of cerebral blood flow are temporary and typically resolve quickly without permanent damage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 831
retrieved_docs.content: central role of peripheral nerves in the pathophysiology of diverse neurologic disorders and underscore the need for precise classification, thorough neurologic evaluation, and targeted management to guide diagnosis, treatment, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 859
retrieved_docs.content: Central nervous system imaging procedures (إجراءات تصوير الجهاز العصبي المركزي) encompass the specific finding of an abnormal brain echo pattern (مخطط صدى الدماغ غير طبيعي), indicating that detailed CNS imaging can identify and characterize abnormal echo results in the brain.
